preamble: The shift from the voiceless labiodental fricative [f] to its voiced counterpart [v] is a common form of voicing lenition. It frequently occurs in intervocalic positions or as a result of grammatical markers that trigger voicing to ease articulation between voiced segments.

examples.note: Historical intervocalic voicing retained in pluralization.
Voicing of the fricative in the plural form.
Historical voicing of Latin [f] to [v] in French.
Old English /f/ was realized as [v] between vowels.
